content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture of the codebase. The issue asks me to replace the single mic button with two distinct buttons:
- Button A = Quick Request (current push-to-talk with VAD)
- Button B = Brainstorm (continuous recording, manual stop)

Let me understand the current behavior:
1. Single mic button - push to talk with VAD (Voice Activity Detection) that auto-stops after 3.5s of silence
2. Wake word detection available

For the new dual-mode UI:
- Button A (Quick Request): Behaves exactly like the current mic button - push to talk, VAD auto-stops


- Button B (Brainstorm): Continuous recording, no silence timeout, manual stop required

I need to implement this by updating the HTML to have two buttons, modifying the JavaScript to track which mode is active and apply the appropriate recording logic to each, and styling them distinctly so users can clearly tell them apart.

For the UI, I'm picturing Button A (Quick Request) on the left with the current red accent color and mic icon, and Button B (Brainstorm) on the right in purple with a brain icon or different styling. I'll place them side by side in the mic section with labels, and the active button will display the recording state.

The key difference in behavior is that brainstorm mode disables the silence timer entirely and only stops when the user manually taps the button again, while quick mode uses the existing VAD logic. I'll track this with an `activeMode` state variable that can be `null`, `'quick'`, or `'brainstorm'`.

For the implementation, I need to update the HTML to replace the single mic button with two separate buttons, adjust the CSS to style them differently and position them side by side, and add the state management logic in JavaScript to handle switching between modes.

Now I'm working through the sizing constraints—with a 480px max-width container and two buttons that need breathing room, I'll need to scale them down slightly or adjust the spacing to fit them comfortably.

I'm settling on equal-sized buttons at 80px with 120px rings, positioned side by side with distinct colors: a red/coral accent for Quick Request and purple for Brainstorm. I'll add labels above or below each button to make the modes clear.

Writing the HTML structure... I'm completing the Brainstorm button with its own ring and icon, then realizing I need to refactor the hardcoded IDs in the JavaScript to use the new button identifiers instead of keeping backward compatibility.

For the layout, I'm deciding between stacking the labels above or below each button pair, settling on a centered status hint at the top with individual button labels positioned either above or below their respective rings.

Now I'm setting up the CSS structure for the dual-mode layout with flexbox to arrange the two buttons side-by-side with proper spacing and alignment.

Still writing styles... Still writing styles...

I'm realizing I need to be careful with how the recording state interacts with the brainstorm mode—the ring pulse animation should work correctly whether we're using the base `.mic-ring.recording` selector or a more specific variant for brainstorm mode. Now I'm setting up the JavaScript logic to handle both recording modes. I need to track which mode is active, update the listening function to accept a mode parameter, and implement different behaviors—quick mode uses the existing VAD and silence timer, while brainstorm mode runs continuously without auto-stop until the recognition engine ends or the user manually stops it.

For brainstorm, when the user stops recording, it should show the transcript bar for manual review rather than auto-sending. Since Chrome's recognition engine fires `onend` periodically, I'll need to restart it automatically to keep the continuous recording going, similar to how the wake word mode handles it.

Setting up state tracking...

I need to handle the brainstorm mode restart logic carefully—when recognition stops in Chrome, I should restart it automatically while preserving the transcript. But I have to be careful with `initRecognition()` since it resets the transcript, so I'll need to save and restore it around the reinitialization.

Actually, a cleaner approach is to use a flag to track when we're in a brainstorm restart, so `onstart` knows not to clear the accumulated transcript in that specific case.

Adding a flag to manage this... I see the wake word recognition handles this more cleanly since it doesn't accumulate transcript, but brainstorm mode needs a different strategy. I'm thinking I should introduce a `_pendingTranscript` variable to preserve the transcript across the reinit cycle—setting it before reinitializing and restoring it after the recognition object is recreated, rather than trying to manage the timing around the `onstart` event.

Actually, I might be overcomplicating this. Let me reconsider whether I even need to call `initRecognition()` during the restart.

The real problem is handling the `InvalidStateError` that can occur when trying to restart. Instead, I'll override the `onstart` handler temporarily to restore the saved transcript when recognition restarts, then use a try-catch to gracefully handle any errors that come up.

Still writing error handling... I'm realizing the `onstart` callback keeps resetting things, so let me use a cleaner approach with an accumulator flag that preserves the transcript across restarts in brainstorm mode, and only resets the UI on a fresh start rather than every restart. Writing the restart logic... Building the UI layout... Writing the waveform icon... Setting up the recording UI state...

Now I'm updating the hint text based on which mode is active—"Listening" for quick mode, "Recording" for brainstorm—and toggling the live transcript visibility. But I'm realizing there's a question about how the passive listening ring should work with two separate buttons instead of one.

Since the wake word flow triggers quick recording, the passive listening state should apply to the quick button's ring. I'll update `startWakeRecognition` to target `quickRing` instead of `micRing`, and make sure `handleWakeWordDetected` calls `startListening('quick')` to route it correctly. The wake hint function also needs adjustment to work with this new structure.

For the hint text, I'm thinking it should stay contextual—empty by default since the buttons are self-explanatory, but show "Say 'Hey Chill' or tap Quick" when wake mode is active, and display "Listening… tap to stop" or "Recording… tap to stop" during actual recording. Now I need to handle the `recognition.onend` callback carefully, especially in brainstorm mode where Chrome might stop the recognition while we're still recording...

the issue is that `isListening` stays true, so we'd accidentally call `finishRecording()` on every auto-stop. I'll need to check the `activeMode` to distinguish between intentional stops and Chrome's automatic ones. Since `activeMode` is module-level, I can reference it directly in the handler when I reinitialize the recognition object. Let me write out the full implementation now, starting with the HTML changes to swap in the two-button layout with the new IDs and waveform icon.

For CSS, I'm keeping the existing mic-btn and mic-ring styles as a base, then adding a flex container for the buttons, wrapping each with its label, styling the brainstorm button with purple gradients and glow effects, and handling the passive listening state carefully so it only applies to the quick button.

Still writing styles... Updating the JavaScript to handle both modes—I need to track which mode is active, manage the brainstorm accumulator and restart state, and adjust the silence timer logic so it only applies in quick mode. The wake word functions and UI update methods will need to reference the correct ring and button elements, and I should make sure `finishRecording` clears the brainstorm state properly. Still writing the recognition handler... Still writing recognition handlers... Updating the UI state for both the quick and brainstorm buttons based on whether they're actively recording, toggling their visual styles and updating their accessibility attributes accordingly.

Updating the microphone hint text to show different messages depending on which mode is recording, and managing the live transcript visibility and initial text state.

Still writing the wake hint logic... Writing event handlers...

Now I'm thinking through the edge cases where mode conflicts could occur. The key insight is that the `!isListening` check acts as a guard to prevent starting a new mode if one's already active, so switching between Quick and Brainstorm modes is blocked while either is running. For the wake word trigger, it needs to interrupt whatever's happening and force Quick mode after a brief delay.

I also need to handle the case where `SpeechRecognition` isn't supported—currently only the mic button gets disabled, but I should disable both the mic and brainstorm buttons...

Disabling both buttons when unsupported...

For the mic-hint text, I'm thinking it should stay empty initially since each button already has its own label below it, and the hint area works better for status messages rather than instructions.

Now I'm working through the CSS sizing for the two-button layout. With 128px buttons side by side plus a gap, that's 288px total, which fits comfortably in the 440px available space on a 480px viewport. But on smaller screens around 360px, I should scale down the button to 76px and the ring to 112px to keep everything responsive. Now I'm ready to implement the changes—I need to update the JavaScript to reference the new button IDs for the quick and brainstorm modes instead of the single mic button, while keeping the shared hint element.

content.text: Now rewrite the JavaScript to implement the dual-mode logic:

content.content: The file /home/gem/project/app.js has been updated successfully.

lines: -const SILENCE_DELAY = 3500; // ms of silence before auto-stop
+const SILENCE_DELAY = 3500; // ms — only used in quick mode
+// Chrome stops continuous recognition periodically; we restart seamlessly in brainstorm mode
+// and preserve accumulated transcript across restarts.
+      // Seamless brainstorm restart — preserve accumulated transcript, don't reset UI
-    // Reset silence timer on every result
+    // Silence timer only applies to quick mode
+      // Chrome stops continuous recognition periodically — restart seamlessly
// onend fires when recognition stops for any reason
// We didn't call stop manually yet; auto-stop happened
// Ensure wake recognition is stopped before starting main recording
isListening = false; // mark before calling stop so onend doesn't re-trigger finishRecording
// Hands-free path: auto-send without requiring a tap
-  micHint.textContent = recording ? 'Tap to stop' : (wakeMode ? 'Say "Hey Chill" or tap' : 'Tap to speak');
+  quickBtn.setAttribute('aria-label', quickActive ? 'Stop recording' : 'Quick Request – tap to speak');
+  brainstormBtn.setAttribute('aria-label', brainstormActive ? 'Stop brainstorm recording' : 'Brainstorm – tap for continuous recording');
wakeTriggeredRecording = false; // manual tap — show transcript bar after
+  quickRing.classList.add('listening-passive'); // passive glow on quick button only
// Give the recognition engine a moment to fully stop before starting main recording
+    startListening('quick'); // wake word always triggers quick mode
-    micHint.textContent = wakeMode ? 'Say "Hey Chill" or tap' : 'Tap to speak';
+    micHint.textContent = wakeMode ? 'Say "Hey Chill" or tap Quick' : '';

input.command: git add index.html app.js style.css && git diff --staged --stat

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(FRG-211): dual-mode UI — Quick Request (A) + Brainstorm (B) buttons

Replace the single mic button with two distinct buttons:

- **Quick (A)**: push-to-talk with VAD silence-detection (3.5 s), same
  behaviour as the previous single button. Retains wake-word integration
  (passive glow, auto-send after "Hey Chill").

- **Brainstorm (B)**: continuous recording with no silence timeout.
  Chrome's periodic recognition restarts are handled transparently by
  accumulating the transcript across restarts. User taps to stop manually,
  then reviews the full transcript in the existing Send / Discard bar.

Visual distinction:
- Quick uses the existing red/accent gradient and pulse ring.
- Brainstorm uses a purple/accent-2 gradient with a waveform-bars icon
  and a matching purple glow animation.
- Both buttons are labeled ("Quick" / "Brainstorm") and sized for
  comfortable side-by-side tap targets on mobile.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

input.command:
gh pr create \
  --title "feat(FRG-211): dual-mode UI — Quick Request + Brainstorm buttons" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
- Replaces the single mic button with two side-by-side buttons: **Quick** (A) and **Brainstorm** (B)
- **Quick** keeps the existing push-to-talk + VAD (3.5 s silence timeout) behaviour and wake-word integration
- **Brainstorm** records continuously with no silence timeout; Chrome's periodic recognition restarts are handled transparently by accumulating the transcript; user taps again to stop and reviews the full transcript before sending

## Visual distinction
- Quick uses the existing red/accent gradient + mic icon + red pulse ring
- Brainstorm uses a purple/accent-2 gradient + waveform-bars icon + purple glow animation
- Both have text labels ("Quick" / "Brainstorm") below each button
- Shared status hint updates to reflect which mode is active

## Key implementation details
- `activeMode` state (`'quick' | 'brainstorm' | null`) replaces the single implicit mode
- `resetSilenceTimer` is a no-op in brainstorm mode
- Brainstorm restart logic: on Chrome's `recognition.onend` mid-session, `brainstormAccumulator` preserves transcript and `_brainstormRestarting` suppresses the `onstart` UI reset
- Wake word always triggers Quick mode

## Test plan
- [ ] Quick: tap Quick → VAD stops after silence → transcript bar appears → Send works
- [ ] Quick: tap Quick → tap again before silence → transcript bar appears
- [ ] Brainstorm: tap Brainstorm → recording continues past 3.5 s → tap again → full transcript shown
- [ ] Both buttons disabled when SpeechRecognition is unsupported
- [ ] Tapping one button while the other is recording does nothing
- [ ] Wake word ("Hey Chill") triggers Quick mode, auto-sends
- [ ] Visual: Quick has red ring when active; Brainstorm has purple ring when active

Closes FRG-211

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

content.text:
## Status
DONE

## What Changed
- `index.html` — replaced single `.mic-ring`/`mic-btn` with two side-by-side buttons: `#quick-btn` (mic icon) and `#brainstorm-btn` (waveform-bars icon), each in a `.mode-btn-wrap` with a label below
- `style.css` — added `.mic-buttons` flex container, `.mode-btn-wrap`/`.mode-label` for layout; added `.brainstorm-btn` purple gradient + recording glow animation; scaled both rings/buttons down slightly for side-by-side fit; added `brainstorm-ring.recording` purple ring override
- `app.js` — introduced `activeMode` state (`'quick'|'brainstorm'|null`); Quick uses existing VAD silence timer, Brainstorm disables it; seamless Brainstorm restart across Chrome's periodic `onend` using `brainstormAccumulator` + `_brainstormRestarting` flag; updated `setRecordingUI`, `updateWakeHint`, wake-word handlers, and not-supported fallback for two buttons

## Key Decisions
- Brainstorm restart preserves accumulated transcript via `brainstormAccumulator` saved in `onend` and restored in `onstart` — avoids a flash/reset of the live transcript area on Chrome's periodic stops
- Wake word always triggers Quick mode (a "Hey Chill" utterance is a focused quick request, not an open brainstorm session)
- Buttons are sized at 76 px (down from 88 px) to fit comfortably side-by-side on a 360 px mobile screen

## PR / Branch
- Branch: `feature/FRG-211-dual-mode-ui-quick-brainstorm`
- PR: https://github.com/chillkimtest-oss/hey-chill/pull/5
